# Transcrição: 06-Promotional Campaign Setup

- **Fonte:** 06-Promotional Campaign Setup
- **Duração:** 03:41
- **Idioma:** en
- **Modelo:** small

---

## [00:00 - 03:03]

When it comes to setting up promotional campaigns, what I like to do is actually separate them out into their own campaigns. What I mean by that is, let's say for example, you're running your standard Facebook campaigns and you have a promotion that's coming up. You've got a 20% offer, it's for three days, and you want to run it at the end of the month. You've got two options here. One is to drop the creative or the new ad sets into an existing campaign and let it run out. Or what I like to do is actually create a separate campaign specifically for promotional purposes only. The advantage of this is that when you're starting new promotions and stopping them, you're not impacting the stability of your existing campaigns. The downside is when you start this off, it can take a bit longer for the data to really kick in. If you go back to understanding how Facebook collects the data and it starts to learn about your customer and who you're targeting, if you fire up a new promotional campaign, then it could take longer for that to kick in. What I do is I don't create new campaigns for every promotion, I simply create a campaign for promotions. Every new promotion we therefore run goes into that promotion's campaign. That campaign itself has spent that it stops and starts as new promotions come and go. But the advantage is my business as usual campaigns, the campaigns that are bringing me the revenue and ROAS, they remain unaffected. At a promotional time, we may reduce budgets of non-promotional campaigns, may even pull some ads and let the promotional campaigns run. But essentially, as you can see here, I'm just showing the campaigns that contain the name promo. So this is the last 30 days of data on this particular account. We've been running promotions all the way through the funnel. So you can see we've got the level one, level two, the level three and level four of the cold, hot, warm and existing customer funnel, the four funnel system. And we're running promotions all the way through. So you can see the spend and return on ad spend, for this account is extremely good so that we know the promotions are working well. But what we're really pushing here is separating out the promotions. And one thing about this particular ad account, when we split that out, had a tremendous positive impact on stability and performance. So if you're running promotions within existing campaigns and ad sets and ads right now, and it's working for you, that's great. My preference is to avoid the impact on my good performing ad sets and campaigns that just continue to run and pull them out. And as you can see here,

## [03:03 - 03:41]

we're able to run different spend levels, optimize within kind of what's going on, and try and kind of push that performance. And we're able to try and separate that out. So it's just one thing to consider when you are running promotions, is that stability side. I'm not against promotions, discounts and things like that. There's definitely a time and place to do it. But certainly when it comes to campaign structure, I prefer to separate them out and create their own four funnel systems specifically for those promotions. And you know, put those strategies that we normally put in place for those promotional purposes as well.
